content:
# Luke Chapter 14 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 106-107 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_014_page_001.jpg, LUK_014_page_002.jpg

---

**1.** And it came-to-pass, when he came into [the] house of a certain [one] of the rulers of the Pharisees on a Sabbath to eat bread, that they were closely-watching him.

**2.** And behold, a certain man who was dropsical was before him.

**3.** And answering, Jesus spoke to the lawyers and Pharisees, saying, "Is it lawful to heal on the Sabbath, or not?"

**4.** But they were silent. And having taken-hold [of him], he healed him and released [him].

**5.** And he said to them, "Which of you, [if] a son or an ox shall fall into a well, will not immediately pull him out on the day of the Sabbath?"

**6.** And they were not able to answer-back to these [things].

**7.** And he was speaking a parable to those having been invited, noticing how they were choosing-out the first-reclining-places, saying to them,

**8.** "When you are invited by someone to a wedding-feast, do not recline in the first-reclining-place, lest someone more honorable than you may have been invited by him,

**9.** and the [one] having invited you and him, coming, shall say to you, 'Give this [one] the place,' and then you should begin with shame to take the last place.

**10.** But when you are invited, going, recline in the last place, so that when the [one] having invited you comes, he shall say to you, 'Friend, go-up higher.' Then there shall be glory to you before all those reclining-together with you.

**11.** Because everyone exalting himself shall be humbled, and the [one] humbling himself shall be exalted."

**12.** And he was saying also to the [one] having invited him, "When you make a dinner or a supper, do not call your friends, nor your brothers, nor your kinsmen, nor [your] rich neighbors, lest they also should invite you in-return, and a repayment be made to you.

**13.** But when you make a feast, invite [the] poor, [the] maimed, [the] lame, [the] blind;

**14.** and you shall be blessed, because they do not have [the means] to repay you; for it shall be repaid to you in the resurrection of the righteous."

**15.** And a certain one of those reclining-together, hearing these [things], said to him, "Blessed [is he] who shall eat bread in the kingdom of God."

**16.** And he said to him, "A certain man was making a great supper, and he invited many.

**17.** And he sent his servant at the hour of the supper to say to those having been invited, 'Come, because [all things] are now ready.'

**18.** And they all from one [accord] began to make-excuse. The first said to him, 'I have bought a field, and I have necessity to go out [and] see it; I ask you, have me excused.'

**19.** And another said, 'I have bought five yoke of oxen, and I am going to prove them; I ask you, have me excused.'

**20.** And another said, 'I have married a wife, and on-account-of this I am not able to come.'

**21.** And the servant, having come, reported these [things] to his lord. Then the master-of-the-house, being angry, said to his servant, 'Go out quickly into the streets and lanes of the city, and bring in here the poor and maimed and blind and lame.'

**22.** And the servant said, 'Lord, what you commanded has been done, and still there is room.'

**23.** And the lord said to the servant, 'Go out into the roads and hedges, and compel [them] to come in, so that my house may be filled.

**24.** For I say to you, that none of those men who have been invited shall taste of my supper.'"

**25.** And great crowds were going-together-with him, and turning, he said to them,

**26.** "If anyone comes to me and does not hate his own father and mother and wife and children and brothers and sisters, and yet also his own life, he is not able to be my disciple.

**27.** Whoever does not bear his own cross and come after me, is not able to be my disciple.

**28.** For which of you, wishing to build a tower, does not first, sitting-down, count the cost, [to see] if he has [enough] for [its] completion?

**29.** Lest, having laid a foundation and not being able to finish, all those beholding should begin to mock him,

**30.** saying, 'This man began to build and was not able to finish.'

**31.** Or what king, going to engage another king in war, will not, sitting-down first, take-counsel [whether] he is able with ten thousand to meet the [one] coming against him with twenty thousand?

**32.** And if not, while he is still far-off, having sent an embassy, he asks [for] the [things] toward peace.

**33.** So therefore, every [one] of you who does not renounce all his own possessions is not able to be my disciple.

**34.** Salt [is] therefore good; but if even the salt should become-tasteless, with what shall it be seasoned?

**35.** It is fit neither for [the] earth nor for [the] dung-heap; they cast it out. The [one] having ears to hear, let him hear."

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **14:1 — "into [the] house of a certain [one] of the rulers of the Pharisees" (εις οικον τινος των αρχοντων των Φαρισαιων):** Sinaiticus reads εις οικον τινος των αρχοντων των Φαρισαιων. The phrase "rulers of the Pharisees" (αρχοντων των Φαρισαιων) does not denote a formal office but identifies a Pharisee who is also a member of the ruling class — possibly a member of the Sanhedrin. "To eat bread" (φαγειν αρτον) is a Semitic idiom for sharing a meal (cf. Genesis 31:54; 2 Samuel 9:7). The detail "they were closely-watching him" (αυτοι ησαν παρατηρουμενοι αυτον) uses the periphrastic imperfect with the middle voice of παρατηρεω, indicating sustained, hostile surveillance — they were watching him for themselves, seeking grounds for accusation (cf. 6:7; 20:20).

2. **14:2 — "dropsical" (υδρωπικος):** Sinaiticus reads υδρωπικος, a New Testament hapax legomenon. The condition (ὕδρωψ, dropsy/edema) involves the pathological accumulation of fluid in body tissues, causing visible swelling. In ancient medical and moral literature, dropsy was sometimes used metaphorically for insatiable desire — Horace (Odes 2.2.13) and Seneca (Epistles 15.10) both employ dropsy as a figure for greed. Whether the man was already present as a deliberate test or appeared providentially, Luke does not specify. The phrase "was before him" (εμπροσθεν αυτου) places the man directly in Jesus' line of sight — an unavoidable confrontation between need and sabbatical regulation.

3. **14:3 — "lawyers and Pharisees" (τους νομικους και Φαρισαιους):** Sinaiticus reads τους νομικους και Φαρισαιους. The νομικοι ("lawyers/experts in the Torah") are distinguished from the Pharisees as a group, though there was significant overlap. Jesus poses his question before acting — "Is it lawful to heal on the Sabbath, or not?" (εξεστιν τω σαββατω θεραπευσαι η ου;) — forcing his opponents to declare themselves. Their silence (ησυχασαν) is strategic: to affirm healing would concede the argument; to deny it would appear cruel before witnesses at a meal. This is the third Sabbath controversy in Luke (cf. 6:6-11; 13:10-17), and each time Jesus' opponents are reduced to silence or shame.

4. **14:5 — "a son or an ox" (υιος η βους):** Sinaiticus reads υιος η βους, "a son or an ox." This is a significant textual variant — many manuscripts read ονος η βους ("a donkey or an ox," echoing 13:15), but Sinaiticus reads υιος ("son"), which creates a more powerful argument from lesser to greater: if you would rescue your own child from a well on the Sabbath, how much more should this man be healed? The pairing of "son" with "ox" is striking in its disparity — the most precious and the merely valuable — and may preserve an earlier, more difficult reading that scribes later harmonized to the more expected "donkey." The well (φρεαρ) was a common hazard in Palestinian terrain; the ruling in the Dead Sea Scrolls (CD 11:13-14) prohibited rescuing an animal from a pit on the Sabbath, making Jesus' argument even more pointed against stricter interpreters.

5. **14:7 — "first-reclining-places" (τας πρωτοκλισιας):** Sinaiticus reads τας πρωτοκλισιας, compound noun from πρωτος ("first") and κλισις ("reclining"). At formal banquets in the Greco-Roman world, guests reclined on couches arranged in a triclinium (three-sided arrangement). The positions closest to the host carried the greatest honor. The verb εξελεγοντο ("they were choosing-out") is imperfect middle — the guests were actively, repeatedly selecting the best places for themselves. Jesus' observation of this social jockeying becomes the occasion for teaching, introduced as a "parable" (παραβολην) — though it reads as practical wisdom, the parabolic label signals deeper meaning about honor in the kingdom.

6. **14:8-10 — The teaching on banquet seating:** This passage draws directly from Proverbs 25:6-7 LXX: "Do not glorify yourself in the presence of a king, and do not stand in the place of the great; for it is better to be told 'Come up here' than to be humbled before a noble." Jesus transforms proverbial wisdom into eschatological instruction. The "wedding-feast" (γαμους) setting elevates the teaching from mere etiquette to a parabolic image of the messianic banquet. The word "friend" (φιλε) in verse 10 — used by the host to honor the humble guest — is the same word used ironically for the betrayer in Matthew 26:50.

7. **14:11 — "everyone exalting himself shall be humbled" (πας ο υψων εαυτον ταπεινωθησεται):** Sinaiticus reads this reversal saying identically to 18:14. The divine passives — ταπεινωθησεται ("shall be humbled") and υψωθησεται ("shall be exalted") — indicate God as the agent of reversal. This is the Magnificat theology in compressed form (1:52, "he has brought down the powerful from [their] thrones and exalted the humble"). The present participle (ο υψων, "the one [habitually] exalting") describes a settled disposition, not a single act.

8. **14:12-14 — Invitation of the poor:** Jesus turns from the guests to the host. The four categories of those to be invited — "poor, maimed, lame, blind" (πτωχους, αναπειρους, χωλους, τυφλους) — reappear in verse 21 as those actually brought to the great supper. These are the people excluded from the Qumran community's messianic banquet (1QSa 2:5-7) and from full Temple participation (cf. 2 Samuel 5:8 LXX). Jesus reverses the exclusion lists of his contemporaries into invitation lists. The phrase "in the resurrection of the righteous" (εν τη αναστασει των δικαιων) presupposes a Pharisaic belief in bodily resurrection (cf. Acts 23:6-8) and locates true recompense beyond the social economy of reciprocal hospitality.

9. **14:15 — "Blessed [is he] who shall eat bread in the kingdom of God" (μακαριος οστις φαγεται αρτον εν τη βασιλεια του θεου):** Sinaiticus reads φαγεται, future middle of εσθιω. This exclamation by a fellow dinner guest is a pious platitude — everyone at the table would agree. The irony is that Jesus' following parable (vv. 16-24) will demonstrate that those who consider themselves guaranteed seats at God's banquet are precisely the ones who will refuse the invitation when it actually comes. The man speaks better than he knows: he pronounces a blessing he himself may be rejecting.

10. **14:16-17 — "a great supper" (δειπνον μεγα):** Sinaiticus reads δειπνον μεγα. The δειπνον was the main meal of the day, taken in the evening. The double invitation was standard social protocol in the ancient Near East: the first invitation was sent in advance to gauge attendance; the second was sent when the meal was prepared, announcing "all things are now ready" (ηδη ετοιμα εστιν). To accept the first invitation and then refuse the second was a grave social insult — the host had prepared food based on confirmed acceptances. The "servant" (δουλον) is singular, unlike Matthew's version (22:3-4) which sends servants (plural) in multiple waves.

11. **14:18-20 — The three excuses:** Sinaiticus preserves three representative excuses, each escalating in the directness of refusal. The first two follow identical syntax: a purchase requiring inspection, followed by the polite formula ερωτω σε εχε με παρητημενον ("I ask you, have me excused"). The third excuse — "I have married a wife" (γυναικα εγημα) — offers no apology at all, simply stating inability. This may echo Deuteronomy 24:5, which exempts a newly married man from military service for one year, but Jesus' parable does not validate the excuse. The purchases of field and oxen represent landed wealth and agricultural enterprise; the marriage represents domestic life. Together they encompass the full range of worldly attachments that compete with the kingdom's summons.

12. **14:21 — "streets and lanes" (τας πλατειας και ρυμας):** Sinaiticus reads τας πλατειας και ρυμας — the wide streets and narrow alleys of the city. This first outreach is within the city walls (representing Israel), gathering the "poor and maimed and blind and lame" — the same four categories from verse 13, now narratively fulfilled. The master's anger (οργισθεις) is a strong emotional term — the insult of mass refusal provokes decisive action to fill the banquet by other means.

13. **14:23 — "roads and hedges" (τας οδους και φραγμους):** Sinaiticus reads τας οδους και φραγμους — the open roads and hedge-lined boundaries outside the city. This second outreach moves beyond the city walls, traditionally understood as extending the invitation to Gentiles. The verb αναγκασον ("compel") does not imply physical coercion but urgent, insistent persuasion — those living along roads and hedges (travelers, the destitute, those without homes) would need convincing that such an invitation was genuine. Augustine later misused this verse to justify religious coercion, but the original context is about the urgency and generosity of divine hospitality overwhelming reluctance born of shame or disbelief.

14. **14:24 — "none of those men" (ουδεις των ανδρων εκεινων):** Sinaiticus reads ουδεις των ανδρων εκεινων. The shift to "I say to you" (λεγω γαρ υμιν) in the plural may indicate Jesus stepping outside the parable to address his dinner companions directly — "you" (υμιν) is plural, but the host in the story would be addressing his single servant. The parable's conclusion is absolute: the original invitees are permanently excluded. There is no third chance. The verb γευσεται ("shall taste") implies experiential participation — they will not so much as sample the feast they were originally invited to enjoy.

15. **14:26 — "hate" (μισει):** Sinaiticus reads ει τις ερχεται προς με και ου μισει, "if anyone comes to me and does not hate." The Semitic usage of μισεω ("hate") here does not denote emotional hostility but a decisive subordination of one loyalty to another — as in Genesis 29:31-33 where Leah is "hated" (meaning loved less) relative to Rachel, and Malachi 1:2-3 ("Jacob I loved, but Esau I hated"). The list of those to be "hated" — father, mother, wife, children, brothers, sisters — encompasses the entire kinship network that defined identity and obligation in the ancient world. The addition of "yet also his own life" (ετι τε και την ψυχην εαυτου) extends the demand beyond social bonds to self-preservation itself. The threefold repetition of "is not able to be my disciple" (ου δυναται ειναι μου μαθητης) in verses 26, 27, and 33 creates a devastating refrain.

16. **14:27 — "bear his own cross" (βασταζει τον σταυρον εαυτου):** Sinaiticus reads βασταζει τον σταυρον εαυτου. The present tense βασταζει ("bears/carries") denotes an ongoing action — cross-bearing is not a single dramatic gesture but a continuous posture of life. In the Roman world, a condemned person carried the horizontal beam (patibulum) of the cross to the place of execution (cf. John 19:17). For Jesus' audience, the image was not metaphorical but literal — crucifixion was a visible, common reality of Roman occupation. To "come after" (ερχεται οπισω) Jesus is to follow in his path, which leads through suffering to God.

17. **14:28-32 — Two parables of calculation:** Sinaiticus preserves two illustrations unique to Luke — the tower-builder and the warring king — both demanding sober assessment before commitment. The tower (πυργον) may be a watchtower for a vineyard (cf. Isaiah 5:2; Mark 12:1) or a farm building; the point is the shame (αρξωνται...εμπαιζειν, "begin to mock") of an unfinished project. The king's scenario introduces the concept of an "embassy" (πρεσβειαν) — diplomatic negotiation from a position of weakness. The verb βουλευεται ("takes counsel/deliberates") in verse 31 shares its root with βουλη, the term for the Sanhedrin's counsel (23:51). Both parables argue from worldly prudence to spiritual decision: if rational people count costs in business and warfare, how much more should one calculate the total cost of discipleship before beginning?

18. **14:33 — "renounce all his own possessions" (αποτασσεται πασιν τοις εαυτου υπαρχουσιν):** Sinaiticus reads αποτασσεται, present middle of αποτασσω ("to take leave of, renounce, say farewell to"). The same verb is used for saying farewell to family in 9:61 and Acts 18:18. Here it is applied to possessions (υπαρχουσιν, literally "the things belonging to [him]"). The present tense again indicates an ongoing disposition, not merely an initial act of divestment. This is the conclusion drawn from the two calculation parables: having counted the cost, the disciple must be prepared to surrender everything. Luke's Gospel consistently emphasizes the danger of wealth (6:20, 24; 12:13-21; 16:19-31; 18:18-25), and this verse states the principle in its most radical form.

19. **14:34 — "if even the salt should become-tasteless" (εαν δε και το αλας μωρανθη):** Sinaiticus reads μωρανθη, aorist passive subjunctive of μωραινω, which means both "to make foolish" (cf. Romans 1:22; 1 Corinthians 1:20) and "to make tasteless/insipid." The double meaning is deliberate: salt that has lost its savor has become foolish. Pure sodium chloride cannot actually lose its saltiness, but the impure salt collected from the shores of the Dead Sea (mixed with gypsum and other minerals) could indeed lose its flavor through exposure to moisture, leaving a tasteless mineral residue useful for nothing. The connection to discipleship is implicit: a disciple who will not bear the cross and renounce all is like salt that has lost its essential character.

20. **14:35 — "neither for [the] earth nor for [the] dung-heap" (ουτε εις γην ουτε εις κοπριαν):** Sinaiticus reads ουτε εις γην ουτε εις κοπριαν. Tasteless salt is doubly useless — it cannot fertilize soil (εις γην) and cannot even be mixed into a manure pile (εις κοπριαν) to enhance decomposition, which was a known agricultural use of salt in the ancient world. It is simply discarded (εξω βαλλουσιν αυτο, "they cast it outside"). The concluding formula, "the one having ears to hear, let him hear" (ο εχων ωτα ακουειν ακουετω), is Jesus' standard call to discernment (cf. 8:8; Matthew 11:15; 13:9, 43; Mark 4:9, 23; Revelation 2:7, 11, 17, 29). It signals that the preceding teaching — on the cost of discipleship and the danger of tasteless commitment — requires not mere hearing but penetrating understanding and decisive response.
